title: The Zero Percent Market
subhead: Nvidia's China collapse marks the moment US export controls forced Beijing to build its own stack. The question now: does Huawei have enough runway to deliver before domestic alternatives fragment? Meanwhile, the Philippines becomes the first territory to fire a Tomahawk from a Typhon launcher — a signal to Beijing that Washington can now put land-based cruise missiles within range of the Taiwan Strait.

sections.heading: Nvidia's China Market Goes to Zero
sections.body:
Nvidia CEO Jensen Huang confirmed this week that the company's share of China's data-center computing market has dropped to zero following US export restrictions — down from 95% just a few years ago. The admission came as Huang called for Washington to allow continued sales of older chips to China while supporting US leadership in cutting-edge AI.

The structural shift: China isn't trying to buy American chips anymore — it's building its own. DeepSeek's V4 model now runs on Huawei's Ascend 950 AI chips, and major Chinese tech firms are scrambling to secure domestic hardware. The export controls didn't preserve US market dominance; they accelerated Chinese self-reliance. Nvidia's $5.2 trillion market cap reflects global AI demand, but the China revenue stream is structurally severed.

Reuters reports Chinese tech firms are rushing to secure Huawei Ascend chips following DeepSeek's V4 launch, with demand surging as Beijing pushes to end reliance on Nvidia. The Supermicro smuggling allegations — $2.5 billion in Nvidia-chipped servers allegedly smuggled to China — illustrate the desperation on both sides of the fence, but the direction is clear: China is betting on Huawei, not workarounds.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move for Beijing: accelerate domestic chip yields, accept initial performance gaps, and iterate. The trap for Washington: assuming Chinese chips will stay behind. If Huawei closes the gap within 2-3 years, the US will have traded market access for competitor capability with no leverage remaining.

sections.heading: Apple Explores Intel and Samsung for US Chip Production
sections.body:
Apple has held exploratory discussions with Intel and Samsung about manufacturing its main device processors in the United States, seeking to diversify beyond TSMC as advanced chip supply remains tight. The move signals growing recognition that concentration in Taiwan — where TSMC produces the majority of Apple's chips — represents an unacceptable geopolitical risk.

Apple's dilemma captures the broader reshoring paradox: TSMC's Arizona fab remains two nodes behind Taiwan, and Intel's foundry business is still proving itself. Samsung's yields on advanced nodes have been inconsistent. Apple isn't choosing between good options; it's choosing between dependency on Taiwan and dependency on second-tier US capacity. The company that defined the smartphone era now faces the same structural constraint everyone else faces: there is no backup for TSMC that matches TSMC.

Reports from MacRumors, Digitimes, and Analytics Insight all confirm Apple's supply chain diversification talks, but no manufacturing commitments have been announced. The exploratory nature of the discussions suggests Apple is testing whether meaningful US capacity can materialize — not that it has.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move for Apple: commit to US production despite yield gaps, accepting higher costs for supply security. The trap: assuming Intel or Samsung can scale before Taiwan faces acute pressure. If Taiwan destabilizes and US capacity isn't ready, Apple becomes a case study in the cost of geopolitical hedging.

sections.heading: US First Tomahawk Launch from Philippines-Based Typhon
sections.body:
The US military test-fired a Tomahawk cruise missile from the Typhon mid-range capability system in the Philippines for the first time, striking a target at Fort Magsaysay approximately 600 kilometers away. The launch, conducted during Balikatan exercises, marks the first operational demonstration of the land-based system that has been deployed in the Philippines since April 2024.

The Typhon launcher carrying Tomahawks in the Philippines transforms the South China Sea into contested space. From Philippine territory, Tomahawks can reach the Taiwan Strait, the South China Sea, and parts of the Chinese mainland. China has repeatedly condemned the deployment, warning of 'geopolitical confrontation and arms race.' The warning is correct: Washington is signaling that it can place intermediate-range conventional missiles on Beijing's periphery, something it couldn't do before the Philippines pivot.

The test involved coordination with Philippine forces and comes ahead of maritime strike drills in Laoag, where defense ministers from the Philippines and Japan will observe. Japan has deployed 1,400 troops for the exercises and will fire its Type 88 missiles alongside US Navy-Marine Expeditionary Ship Interdiction System.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move for Washington: normalize permanent missile presence on Taiwan's southern flank. The trap for Beijing: responding with escalation that justifies further US deployment. If China militarizes its artificial islands further, Washington gains more invitations to place Typhon systems in more locations.

sections.heading: EU Moves to Exclude Huawei and ZTE from 5G Networks
sections.body:
The European Commission has recommended that member states exclude Huawei and ZTE equipment from telecom infrastructure, citing security risks. The guidance, while not legally binding, signals a hardening European position on Chinese network infrastructure — following years of debate and partial restrictions across individual member states.

Every Huawei 5G installation creates a structural dependency. The EU recommendation recognizes that network infrastructure isn't neutral equipment — it's the data backbone that determines who can observe, intercept, or disrupt communications. Brussels is finally aligning with Washington's earlier hard line, but implementation will vary across 27 member states with different Huawei penetration levels.

Cybernews reports that China has threatened countermeasures over 'discriminatory' EU cybersecurity rules, indicating Beijing views network infrastructure exclusion as an escalatory step. The EU is also moving to ban renewable energy projects using Chinese components when funded by European funds.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move for Europe: implement a unified 5G infrastructure that removes Chinese dependency within 5 years, accepting short-term costs for long-term autonomy. The trap: partial implementation that leaves strategic vulnerabilities in place while antagonizing Beijing.

sections.heading: Russia Deploys Fiber-Optic Drones to Kramatorsk
sections.body:
Russian fiber-optic FPV drones have reached Kramatorsk, one of Ukraine's most fortified cities in Donetsk Oblast, marking a significant extension of this technology's threat range. The Kyiv Independent reports that these drones — immune to radio frequency jamming — are now operating in urban environments previously considered safer from drone attacks.

Fiber-optic drones represent the next evolution in electronic warfare: they cannot be jammed because they're connected by cable. Russia sources most fiber-optic spools directly from China, while Ukrainian drone makers rely on intermediaries that result in less reliable and shorter-range optical fiber. The supply chain asymmetry matters — China isn't selling to Ukraine what it sells to Russia.

The drones are particularly effective in urban environments where buildings block radio signals but fiber-optic cables can navigate around obstacles. Kramatorsk serves as a staging area for Ukrainian units in the Donbas, making this capability extension tactically significant.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move for Ukraine: develop domestic fiber-optic production and anti-drone tactics that don't rely on jamming. The trap: assuming electronic warfare superiority persists against a wired adversary. If Russia can scale fiber-optic production, the drone defense calculus changes fundamentally.

sections.heading: China Signals J-35A Export Variant for Pakistan
sections.body:
China's state broadcaster CCTV has revealed the first export variant of the J-35A stealth fighter, dubbed the J-35AE, with Pakistan identified as the likely first customer. The aircraft shown bore the AVIC logo rather than PLA Air Force markings, confirming its export designation.

China's export of fifth-generation fighters to Pakistan represents a structural shift in South Asian air power. India faces the prospect of a neighbor fielding stealth aircraft that can penetrate its air defense network. More importantly, China is signaling that it can and will export advanced platforms to partners, building military interoperability and dependency in ways the US has done for decades.

The SCMP reports that the J-35AE features an internally integrated electro-optical targeting system, indicating stealth maturity rather than prototype status. Hong Kong military analyst Liang Guoliang assessed the aircraft as a 'complete product ready for export.'
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move for China: establish Pakistan as a long-term defense customer with ongoing maintenance, training, and upgrade dependency. The trap for India: responding with its own fifth-generation acquisition without the industrial base to sustain it, creating strategic dependency on a different set of foreign suppliers.

sections.heading: France Convenes G7 on Critical Minerals
sections.body:
France has called an online meeting of G7 countries for Thursday to discuss breaking China's stranglehold on critical minerals. Finance Minister Roland Lescure announced plans to rebuild the rare earth industry in France, recognizing that critical minerals have replaced oil as the most contested resource for advanced economies.

China's rare earth dominance isn't a geological accident — it's a decades-long industrial strategy. China controls 70%+ of rare earth mining and 90%+ of processing. When Beijing restricted gallium and germanium exports in 2023, it demonstrated that mineral export controls work faster than semiconductor export controls: no fab is built without germanium, no LED without gallium.

The Forbes analysis notes that critical minerals are 'the new center of global competition.' Zimbabwe risks losing its lithium edge as global demand intensifies, while Australia debates whether green demand will push transition metals into a new supercycle. France's G7 initiative seeks coordinated Western response to Chinese mineral leverage.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move for the West: build processing capacity, not just mines. China's advantage is in refining, not mining. The trap: subsidizing mines without processing infrastructure, creating dependency on Chinese refining for Western ores.

sections.heading: Türkiye: Defense Exports and the Steel Dome
sections.body:
Turkish defense company Pasifik Teknoloji announced a deal to export 100,000 FPV kamikaze drones to an unnamed 'friendly and allied' country — its first major defense export. The package includes Alpin helicopters, Dumrul mini helicopters, and Korgan autonomous ground units. Meanwhile, Aselsan will ramp up delivery of Steel Dome air defense components in 2026, and the SAHA 2026 defense expo in Istanbul expects $8 billion in deal volume.

Türkiye is positioning itself as a defense exporter to countries that cannot access or cannot afford US/NATO equipment. The Bayraktar TB2 demonstrated this model in Ukraine; now Pasifik is scaling it for loitering munitions. If Türkiye can deliver cost-effective alternatives to Western platforms, it builds defense dependency relationships that outlast individual conflicts.

Daily Sabah reports Türkiye aims to become one of the top 10 defense and aerospace exporters, reducing foreign dependency in critical systems while deepening global partnerships.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move for Türkiye: establish itself as the alternative defense supplier for countries seeking autonomy from great power alignment. The trap: building export dependency without the sustainment infrastructure that keeps customers locked in long-term.

sections.heading: Ukraine-Taiwan Drone Knowledge Transfer
sections.body:
The New York Times reports on an emerging informal network connecting Ukrainian drone operators and Taiwanese volunteers. Despite the absence of official diplomatic ties — Ukraine adheres to the One China policy — company executives and defense volunteers are sharing lessons on drone warfare technology and tactics.

Taiwan faces a structural problem: it needs to learn drone warfare from Ukraine, but Ukraine depends on Chinese components for its drone industry. Both Taiwan and Ukraine are deeply integrated with the Chinese economy, creating knowledge transfer pathways that run through supply chain dependencies neither can fully sever.

Taiwanese volunteers are serving in Ukraine's military, gaining combat drone experience they hope to apply in a future conflict with China. The informal nature of the relationship allows both governments to maintain their official positions while enabling practical cooperation.
sections.whyItMatters: The decisive move for Taiwan: accelerate domestic drone production and reduce component dependency on China. The trap: assuming knowledge transfer can substitute for industrial capacity when the shooting starts.

bottomLine: The zero-percent moment has arrived: US export controls have successfully forced China to build its own semiconductor and AI stack. The question now is whether Huawei can deliver before Chinese alternatives fragment into competing platforms, or whether Beijing's unified industrial policy proves more effective than Washington's fragmented corporate landscape. Meanwhile, the Philippines Typhon launch and J-35AE export signal confirm that the great power competition is now about placement — where missiles can reach, where fighters can fly, and where supply chains can be severed. The countries that control the chokepoints — rare earth processing, fiber-optic production, stealth fighter maintenance — will define the next decade. Those who assumed the chokepoints would remain stable are learning otherwise.
sources: Reuters, Nikkei Asia, SCMP, Kyiv Independent, Naval News, Daily Sabah, Anadolu Agency, NY Times, Forbes, Cybernews, MSN/Bing News, Yahoo Finance, Digitimes, MacRumors
